title: Structured-subscripts regression: account-update REWRITE no longer persists (commit 57cf80e9)
description:
Task 7 verification of the structured-subscripts slice (docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-09-structured-subscripts.md) found a REGRESSION.

## Symptom
After rebuilding the proleap-bridge JAR and running the gated CardDemo CICS e2e suite, 3 tests FAIL at HEAD (e316046a):
- tests/integration/cics/test_carddemo_signon_real.py::test_real_carddemo_account_update_rewrite
- tests/integration/cics/test_vsam_persistence.py::test_account_update_rewrite_persists_to_flat_file
- tests/integration/cics/test_vsam_dump_e2e.py::test_dump_decodes_rewritten_acctdat_status_n

Failure: REWRITE does not persist the status change — stored ACCTDAT active-status byte stays 'Y', expected 'N'. assert 'Y' == 'N'.

## Bisect (conclusive)
- Baseline 40825456 (pre-slice): 3 tests PASS.
- 1a99c445 (bridge emits structured subscripts): account_update_rewrite PASSES.
- 57cf80e9 (retire _SUBSCRIPT_RE / parse_subscript_notation): account_update_rewrite FAILS.
- Confirmed by file-revert: copying emit_context.py + field_resolution.py from 1a99c445 on top of HEAD makes the test PASS again; restoring HEAD versions makes it FAIL. Working tree restored clean.

## Root cause locus
interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py resolve_field_ref subscript branch (commit 57cf80e9). The old code, when 'subscripts' was empty, called parse_subscript_notation(name) to recover (base_name, subscript) from legacy 'NAME(SUB)' string notation; the new code unconditionally sets base_name=name, subscript=''. Some operand in the account-update REWRITE lowering path still relies on that string-notation parse (subscript silently dropped now), so the wrong byte offset is written and the status change does not land. Note: in-process monkeypatch instrumentation was inconclusive because pytest runs under xdist workers (-n in addopts); the file-revert bisect is the reliable signal.

## Fix direction (NOT applied — verification-only task, no production edits)
Either (a) restore the legacy NAME(SUB) string parse for the transitional empty-subscripts case in emit_context.py, OR (b) trace the specific REWRITE-path feeder that still emits string-notation operands and convert it to structured subscripts (the truly structural fix per red-dragon-6ddr). Recommend (b). Identify the feeder by tracing the account-update program's REWRITE operands through lowering.

## Other verification results (all clean)
- grep -rn '_SUBSCRIPT_RE|parse_subscript_notation' interpreter/ -> EMPTY (regex fully retired).
- black --check interpreter/ tests/ -> all clean (789 files).
- lint-imports -> 5 contracts kept, 0 broken.
- Remaining CICS suite: 35 passed (account-view, transaction-add, PROGRAM(...) subscript menu dispatch all green).
close_reason: Closed

title: CICS/COBOL field references are stringly-typed: parser flattens subscript/ref-mod structure to a string, re-parsed downstream by regex
description:
STRUCTURAL DEBT (P0). A reference-modified / subscripted operand is parsed STRUCTURALLY by the grammar, then immediately flattened back to a string, then re-parsed by regex downstream — a structure -> string -> structure round-trip.

CONCRETE PATH (CICS example, but the convention is codebase-wide):
- interpreter/cics/cics_parser.py value() (~line 100): the recursive value/vnested grammar parses PROGRAM(CDEMO-MENU-OPT-PGMNAME(WS-OPTION)) into nested parts, then does ''.join(p.text for p in items) -> the string 'CDEMO-MENU-OPT-PGMNAME(WS-OPTION)'.
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py resolve_field_ref (~line 198) calls parse_subscript_notation(name).
- interpreter/cobol/field_resolution.py parse_subscript_notation (line 15) uses a regex _SUBSCRIPT_RE.match to recover (base_name, subscript) from the string.

So nested-paren structure the grammar already had is destroyed and re-derived by regex. Same applies to reference-modification WS-MSG(1:8) (subscript='1:8' then split again downstream).

WHY P0 / SYSTEMIC: parse_subscript_notation + _SUBSCRIPT_RE is the SHARED COBOL field resolver used by ALL 15 frontends — field references throughout the codebase are passed as strings like FOO(BAR) and FOO(start:len). The regex handles only limited nesting/forms and silently defaults (resolve_field_ref logs 'Subscript field ... defaulting to 1' on miss). This stringly-typed field-reference contract is fragile (multi-level subscripts FOO(I,J) for 2D tables, nested FOO(BAR(BAZ)), ref-mod-with-expression FOO(I+1:LEN), qualified X OF Y combined with subscripts) and is the kind of regex re-parse the project bans.

DESIRED END STATE: field references carried as a STRUCTURED type (base + structured subscript list + optional ref-mod start/length as expression nodes) end-to-end, from each frontend's parse through resolve_field_ref, with NO string round-trip and NO regex re-parse. The CICS parser already HAS the structure (recursive value/vnested) and should hand it over structurally instead of joining to a string. This is a cross-frontend refactor of the shared field-resolution contract (field_resolution.py, emit_context.resolve_field_ref, and every caller that passes 'NAME(SUB)' strings), so it is large and must be planned (brainstorm + spec + plan).

SCOPE NOTE: deferred deliberately; addressing later. Filed at user request as P0 to mark the structural debt. Surfaced while folding CICS-parser regex/string hacks into the grammar (envelope strip + compound verbs handled separately).
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: COPY copybook inlining broken — stdin invocation defeats ProLeap resolution
description:
PREREQUISITE / BLOCKER for any real-world COBOL (batch AND online CardDemo). Copybook (COPY) inlining does not work, and an unresolvable COPY is a HARD PARSE FAILURE, not a graceful skip.

ROOT CAUSE (empirically confirmed):
- Our pipeline always pipes source via stdin: ProLeapCobolParser.parse(source: bytes) -> RealSubprocessRunner -> java -jar bridge, source on stdin. The project compiler does file_path.read_bytes() then the same stdin path.
- The bridge (Main.java resolveInputFile) writes stdin to a throwaway temp file via Files.createTempFile -> system temp dir (java.io.tmpdir).
- ProLeap's CobolParserRunnerImpl.createDefaultParams sets copyBookDirectories = [cobolFile.getParentFile()] = the SYSTEM TEMP DIR, which never contains the program's copybooks.
- Result: 'CobolPreprocessorException: Could not find copy book MYBOOK in directory of COBOL input file or copy books param object.' -> entire parse fails.

extract_imports (the MULTI_FILE_IMPORTS feature) only DETECTS COPY refs via regex for the dependency graph; it never inlines. No setCopyBookDirectories plumbing is exposed from Python.

ProLeap itself supports COPY expansion correctly — it just needs the copybook directory. The gap is purely in HOW we invoke it.

FIX OPTIONS (decide when scheduled; do not pre-commit):
(a) Invoke the bridge with a real source FILE PATH (project compiler writes/uses on-disk paths) so ProLeap's existing parent-dir resolution finds co-located copybooks. Requires parse() to accept a path, not just bytes.
(b) Add a -copybook-dir arg to the bridge that calls setCopyBookDirectories(...), and plumb a copybook search-path list from the Python frontend (parse interface gains a copybook_dirs param).
Either way: bounded change to the bridge invocation + the parse() interface. Must preserve the existing stdin path or migrate callers.

IMPACT: blocks parsing of essentially all real COBOL with copybooks — every CardDemo program (COCOM01Y, BMS symbolic maps, DFHAID, DFHBMSCA, record layouts) and most batch programs. The CICS epic ([deleted:red-dragon-pz9g]) implicitly assumed working COPY resolution (e.g. shipping DFHAID/DFHBMSCA on a copybook path).
acceptance_criteria: 1. A program with COPY BOOK referencing a co-located copybook parses successfully and the copybook's data items appear in the ASG (e.g. WS-FROM-COPY resolvable). 2. Multi-copybook and COPY ... OF library forms resolve. 3. A genuinely missing copybook produces a clear, surfaced error (not a silent wrong result). 4. The copybook search path is configurable from the Python frontend. 5. Unit + integration tests: COPY inlining end-to-end (data item from copybook used in PROCEDURE DIVISION and executed). 6. Existing stdin-based callers still work (or are migrated).

title: COBOL: INITIALIZE on a group item does not reset its child fields
description:
INITIALIZE applied to a group item name may not iterate over the group's flattened child fields. The layout is a flat FieldLayout map; group items appear as entries but the INITIALIZE lowering code iterates over stmt.operands and resolves each name directly. If the name maps to the group-level entry rather than to each child, child fields are not reset.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py (INITIALIZE block, ~line 271-281) calls emit_field_encode for each operand. A group item's FieldLayout entry has a computed byte_length covering all children, but INITIALIZE should recursively reset each leaf field to its type-appropriate default (spaces for alphanumeric, zeros for numeric), not write a blanket byte range.

Example:
  01 WS-REC.
    05 WS-A PIC X(3).
    05 WS-B PIC 9(4).
  INITIALIZE WS-REC.
WS-A should be spaces, WS-B should be zeros. Writing the group entry as a single region with a mixed default is incorrect.

Affected file: interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py (~line 264-281).
acceptance_criteria:
1. INITIALIZE of a group item sets each alphanumeric child to spaces and each numeric child to zeros.
2. Nested group items are recursively reset (grandchildren, etc.).
3. Integration test: group with mixed PIC X and PIC 9 children; INITIALIZE group; assert each child has type-correct default.
4. INITIALIZE of a leaf field continues to work correctly.
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: INITIALIZE on a redefining field clobbers the redefined field's bytes
description:
INITIALIZE applied to a REDEFINES field correctly identifies the field's offset in the layout, but because REDEFINES fields share the same byte offset as the field they redefine, INITIALIZE overwrites the shared byte range with spaces or zeros, destroying whatever the redefined field held.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py (INITIALIZE lowering, ~line 264-281) looks up the FieldLayout for the operand by name and emits a WriteRegion at that field's offset and byte_length. Since REDEFINES fields share an offset (data_layout.py:75-79), this has the side-effect of also initializing the redefined field's bytes.

Example:
  01 WS-NUM PIC 9(5) VALUE 12345.
  01 WS-CHARS REDEFINES WS-NUM PIC X(5).
  INITIALIZE WS-CHARS.
After INITIALIZE WS-CHARS, WS-NUM should still read as 12345, but its bytes have been space-filled.

Related: data_layout.py:228-268 (_fix_redefines_offsets), lower_arithmetic.py INITIALIZE block.
acceptance_criteria:
1. INITIALIZE of a redefining field resets only that field's view (space-fills alphanumeric, zero-fills numeric).
2. After INITIALIZE WS-CHARS (alphanumeric overlay), reading WS-NUM (original numeric) still returns its pre-INITIALIZE value — no byte corruption.
3. Integration test: write a known value to original field, INITIALIZE the redefining field, assert original is unchanged.
4. INITIALIZE of the original field correctly resets the shared bytes.
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: all arithmetic uses Python float — silent precision loss for fixed-point fields
description:
Every COBOL arithmetic operation (ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE, COMPUTE) routes through a Python float (IEEE 754 double, ~15-17 significant digits) as the intermediate value. Fields with PIC clauses requiring more than 15 combined integer+decimal digits silently lose precision. No error is raised; the truncated value is re-encoded back into the field.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py:103-110 constructs a Binop IR instruction that operates on the decoded float values. emit_context.py:394-444 (emit_decode_field) decodes all numeric categories to float before arithmetic. The encoding back in emit_encode_numeric (emit_context.py:341-392) operates on the post-rounded float result.

Example: PIC S9(15)V9(10) contains 25 significant digits. After one ADD operation, the result is rounded to 15-17 digits, corrupting the last 8-10 decimal places. The COBOL standard requires exact fixed-point arithmetic.

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py:103-110
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py:341-444
- interpreter/cobol/binary.py:78-105 (decode returns float)
- interpreter/cobol/comp3.py:83-125 (decode returns float)
- interpreter/cobol/zoned_decimal.py:73-105 (decode returns float)
acceptance_criteria:
1. COMPUTE x = 0.1 + 0.2 where x is PIC 9V99 stores exactly 0.30 (not 0.30000000000000004).
2. A field PIC 9(10)V9(10) survives 100 ADD 0.0000000001 TO field operations and holds the correct exact value.
3. All existing COBOL arithmetic integration tests continue to pass.
4. No decode function returns Python float for fixed-point categories; all return exact integer-scaled values.

title: CI: import-linter fails — stale module paths in .importlinter
description: .importlinter references interpreter.executor and interpreter.backend which were reorganized to interpreter.vm.executor and interpreter.llm.backend. lint-imports fails in CI with 'Module interpreter.executor does not exist.'
close_reason: Updated interpreter.executor → interpreter.vm + interpreter.handlers, interpreter.backend → interpreter.llm. Added ignore for pre-existing symbol_table import. Both contracts pass.

title: VM scope chain: STORE in called function doesn't propagate writes to caller/enclosing scope
description: STORE inside a called function always writes to the current StackFrame local_vars. When the frame is popped on return, the write is lost. This affects ALL languages: Python global, JS closure writes, Java/Kotlin/Scala static field mutation from methods, Go package-level vars, C/C++ globals. Root cause: _handle_store only writes to vm.current_frame without checking parent frames. Workaround: self.field / this.field uses STORE_FIELD which writes to the heap (works correctly). This is why Rosetta bubble_sort excludes Scala — the method can't modify the object's arr field.
design: Approach: Add DECL_VAR opcode to IR. DECL_VAR always creates in current frame. STORE_VAR walks scope chain, updates first match, creates local if not found. Frontends emit DECL_VAR for declarations (let/var/val/int x = ...) and STORE_VAR for assignments (x = ...). Tree-sitter already distinguishes these as variable_declaration vs assignment_expression. VM change is small: _handle_store_var walks reversed call_stack checking local_vars for existing variable.

title: P0: test_function_type_subtype_of_any asserts the opposite of its name
description: In test_type_graph.py, test_function_type_subtype_of_any asserts 'not is_subtype_expr()' but name/docstring say 'subtype of Any'. Name and docstring are inverted — should be test_function_type_not_subtype_of_any.

title: Shared access-method storage engine (neutral outcomes, mainframe-stack-shared)
description: One faithful COBOL file-I/O engine in red-dragon, shared by the mainframe stack (jackal/cicada/squall), replacing the 3 divergent paths: COBOL RealFileIOProvider, jackal IDCAMS (direct driver imports), cicada's separate VsamEngine. CRUX: the engine returns a NEUTRAL access-method outcome (the underlying condition: OK/EOF/NOT_FOUND/DUPLICATE_KEY/IO_ERROR/...), NOT COBOL FILE STATUS or EIBRESP — each consumer's thin adapter maps it to its vocabulary. That neutral contract is what makes it shareable (today's IOResult bakes in COBOL FILE STATUS, which is why cicada wrote its own engine). FIRST PIECE = behavior-preserving consolidation: neutral engine over the existing PS/KSDS/RRDS drivers + factory; refactor the COBOL CobolIOProvider onto it (neutral->FILE STATUS, output identical); route jackal IDCAMS through it. Full red-dragon + jackal suites are the guard. DEFERRED follow-ons: observability/tracing; fidelity hardening (status-code gaps, LRECL/record-format bugs); cicada VsamEngine merge (a real canonical-architecture decision — file-resident vs in-memory+backend — over the SHARED on-disk flat-record format; the FileOrganizationDriver Protocol stays architecture-neutral so both can implement it); jackal catalog (jackal-vxb, DSN/DISP/GDG/PDS); ESDS/AIX/PDS drivers; squall. Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-25-shared-access-method-engine-design.md. Constraints: FP/frozen, no None defaults, no defensive guards (fail loud), no regex, language-agnostic at the engine layer.
notes: FIRST PIECE MERGED to main (e4fc8f50), 2026-06-25. Neutral AccessResult/AccessCondition + drivers return it + open_driver factory + COBOL adapter maps neutral->FILE STATUS (IOResult unchanged, VM-facing). Behavior-preserving: full suite 14320 passed; final whole-branch review clean. Plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-25-shared-access-method-engine.md. REMAINING in epic (deferred follow-ons): jackal IDCAMS routing (Task 5, next, in jackal after submodule bump); fidelity hardening; cicada VsamEngine merge + CICS adapter; jackal catalog (jackal-vxb); ESDS/AIX/PDS drivers; observability/tracing. KNOWN NOTE: open_driver else->SequentialDriver is not fail-loud (behavior-preserving now; revisit when IDCAMS becomes a 2nd consumer).

title: COBOL: SORT / MERGE / RELEASE / RETURN verbs not lowered (entirely absent)
description:
SORT, MERGE, RELEASE, and RETURN are not handled at all — no bridge serialization, no Python statement dataclass/lowering, and not enumerated in CobolFeature. SORT (file/table sort, often with INPUT/OUTPUT PROCEDURE + RELEASE/RETURN) is ubiquitous in batch COBOL, so this is a significant coverage gap.

PARSER READINESS: ProLeap ALREADY parses these — io.proleap.cobol.asg.metamodel.procedure.StatementTypeEnum defines SORT, MERGE, RELEASE, RETURN. So the work is bridge serialization + Python lowering, not grammar.

EVIDENCE:
- Bridge: no newStatement("SORT"|"MERGE"|"RELEASE"|"RETURN") in StatementSerializer.java (the 41 emitted types are 1:1 with the Python dispatch table; none of these four appear).
- Python: no SortStatement/MergeStatement/etc. in interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py; not in _DISPATCH_TABLE.
- Enum: interpreter/cobol/features.py has no SORT/MERGE/RELEASE/RETURN members — so scripts/feature_coverage_audit.py reports 0 uncovered for them (blind spot; the enum under-enumerates the language).

SCOPE / FORMS:
- SORT file-name ON ASCENDING/DESCENDING KEY ... USING ... GIVING ... (sort-merge file work)
- SORT ... INPUT PROCEDURE IS para [THRU para] OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS para
- RELEASE record [FROM ident] (inside INPUT PROCEDURE)
- RETURN file-name [INTO ident] [AT END ...] (inside OUTPUT PROCEDURE)
- MERGE file-name ON KEY ... USING f1 f2 ... GIVING/OUTPUT PROCEDURE
- SORT table-name (in-memory table sort, COBOL 2002+) — optional, lower priority

REMEDIATION:
1. Add CobolFeature enum members (SORT_STATEMENT, MERGE_STATEMENT, RELEASE_STATEMENT, RETURN_STATEMENT) so coverage tracks them.
2. Bridge: serializeSort/serializeMerge/serializeRelease/serializeReturn emitting keys, sort-keys (name+ASC/DESC), USING/GIVING files, INPUT/OUTPUT PROCEDURE paragraph refs.
3. Python dataclasses + dispatch + lowering (a basic implementation can collect records, sort by keys, feed OUTPUT PROCEDURE / GIVING file).
4. Integration tests through run(): USING/GIVING sort with ASCENDING + DESCENDING keys; INPUT/OUTPUT PROCEDURE form with RELEASE/RETURN.

Found in COBOL language-coverage audit 2026-06-22.

title: COBOL: EVALUATE with ALSO (multiple subjects) silently drops all subjects after the first
description:
SYMPTOM: EVALUATE with multiple ALSO subjects/objects is silently truncated — only the first subject is evaluated.

ROOT CAUSE: EvaluateStatement.subject (cobol_statements.py:400) is a single str field. The bridge serialises multi-subject EVALUATE as {"subject": "a ALSO b", "children": [...]} or similar, but the lowering in lower_procedure.py only uses the first subject, ignoring the ALSO clauses.

COBOL form:
  EVALUATE a ALSO b
    WHEN x ALSO y
      ...
    WHEN OTHER
      ...
  END-EVALUATE

This evaluates as: (a == x) AND (b == y). Each WHEN clause has a matching list of ALSO-separated values. Currently only (a == x) is tested.

SCOPE:
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py — EvaluateStatement needs subject to be list[str] or similar to hold multiple subjects
- interpreter/cobol/lower_procedure.py — evaluate lowering must AND together all per-subject comparisons in each WHEN branch
- proleap-bridge serializer — verify the JSON shape for multi-subject EVALUATE (may emit "subjects": [...] or similar)

EXAMPLE:
  EVALUATE WS-STATUS ALSO WS-FLAG
    WHEN "OK" ALSO "Y"
      PERFORM HAPPY-PATH
    WHEN "ERR" ALSO ANY
      PERFORM ERROR-PATH
    WHEN OTHER
      PERFORM UNKNOWN
  END-EVALUATE
close_reason: Implemented: bridge serializes also_subjects/also_conditions; lower_evaluate ANDs each subject=condition pair via BINOP &&, ANY skips a dimension. 4 e2e tests in TestEvaluateAlso (commit 18897b04). Pushed.

title: COBOL: multi-dimensional array subscripts raise NotImplementedError
description:
SYMPTOM: Any access to a 2D+ COBOL table (OCCURS … OCCURS …) raises NotImplementedError at runtime.

ROOT CAUSE: `emit_context.py:265-268` explicitly raises:
  if len(subscripts) > 1:
      raise NotImplementedError(
          f"multi-dimensional subscript not supported for {name!r} "
          f"({len(subscripts)} subscripts); see red-dragon-cqwx"
      )

The offset arithmetic for a 2D subscript is:
  offset = base_offset + (i-1)*outer_stride + (j-1)*inner_stride
where outer_stride = (count_inner * item_size) and inner_stride = item_size.

SCOPE:
- File: interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py (look for multi-dimensional subscript block)
- Bridge: the ProLeap bridge serialises subscripts as a list; the list already has 2 elements for TABLE(i,j) — the bridge side is complete.
- Lowering: need to fold the offset arithmetic for each subscript dimension.

EXAMPLE COBOL:
  01 MY-TABLE.
     05 ROW OCCURS 5 TIMES.
        10 COL OCCURS 3 TIMES.
           15 CELL PIC 9(4).
  MOVE 1 TO CELL (2, 3).

RELATED: red-dragon-cqwx (referenced in the NotImplementedError message)
close_reason: Closed

title: WRITE of a record containing COMP-3 fails: record bytes not latin-1 encodable
description: Found by spike running real batch CBACT01C. WRITE OUT-ACCT-REC (record contains OUT-ACCT-CURR-CYC-DEBIT USAGE COMP-3) throws UnicodeEncodeError: 'latin-1' codec can't encode character '\ufffd' in interpreter/cobol/real_file_provider.py _write_record (data.encode('latin-1')). The record->str assembly for a record containing packed-decimal (COMP-3) produces U+FFFD (replacement char) at the COMP-3 field offset, so byte fidelity is lost before the file write. Root cause is upstream of _write_record (record region bytes -> str conversion for COMP-3), not the encode call itself. Affects ALL batch programs writing packed-decimal output records (pervasive).
acceptance_criteria: A record containing COMP-3 (and COMP/binary) fields WRITEs byte-accurate packed data to a sequential/VSAM file; round-trip read-back decodes equal; integration test writing+reading a COMP-3 record.
notes: DONE 2026-06-20, merged to main (de839f35, commit 544e8e75). Byte-faithful file I/O: WRITE/REWRITE/READ ship raw EBCDIC record-region bytes via CobolEncoding.LATIN1 + emit_read_region_raw/emit_write_region_raw, no EBCDIC<->ASCII translation on the record path. COMP-3 survives WRITE->READ (new round-trip test). User decision: full EBCDIC migration (files now hold raw EBCDIC). 3 ASCII-asserting fixtures migrated to EBCDIC. Full suite 14285 + CardDemo CICS e2e green.

title: Wire USE declaratives to CLOSE errors (CLOSE emits no file status)
description: Follow-up to red-dragon-m0oa.4 (USE AFTER ERROR/EXCEPTION). lower_close in interpreter/cobol/lower_io.py emits the __cobol_close_file call but discards the IOResult: it computes no status_reg and never calls emit_file_status_update. Consequently a USE declarative cannot fire on a CLOSE error (e.g. NIST SQ103A's 'I-O STATUS AFTER CLOSE INDICATES FAILURE' / 'CLOSE FILE FROM OUTPUT/INPUT' assertions still fail). Fix: have lower_close capture the close status into a status_reg, call emit_file_status_update, and call emit_use_trigger(ctx, filename, status_reg, has_explicit_clause=False, materialised) inside its per-file loop (CLOSE has no AT END/INVALID KEY clauses). Verify with the nist_ccvs_tracer on SQ103A: the 3 remaining CLOSE-status assertions should clear.
acceptance_criteria: lower_close produces a file status and fires the matching USE on a CLOSE error; SQ103A CLOSE-status assertions resolved; existing USE + IO tests stay green.
notes:
ESCALATED P3->P1 by spike (2026-06-20): running real batch CBACT01C, the missing CLOSE FILE STATUS update causes a real ABEND at program end. After EOF (status '10'), CLOSE ACCTFILE leaves ACCTFILE-STATUS='10' (lower_close never updates it), program sees status<>'00' and CALLs CEE3ABD. This is load-bearing for real batch, not a NIST tail edge case.
DONE 2026-06-20, merged to main (de839f35, commit 354fdee6). lower_close now extracts __cobol_io_status, calls emit_file_status_update, and fires emit_use_trigger on a non-success close. CBACT01C no longer ABENDs at CLOSE. Full suite + CardDemo CICS e2e green.

title: Subscripted field access on occurrence >=2 drops the (sub-1)*stride term in some lowering paths -> reads/writes occurrence 1; infinite loops
description:
Root cause of the NIST-85 'header-only halt' programs (red-dragon-m0oa.7). Found 2026-06-18.

SYMPTOM: 18 NIST programs print only the CCVS header then never finish — confirmed GENUINE INFINITE LOOPS (RL104A: still 13 print records, no END OF TEST, at 3,000,000 steps; hot block para_REL-TEST-009-R). 8 of the 18 are relative-file (RL) tests, which use the '(2)' record work area.

MINIMAL REPRO (one paragraph):
  01 REC.
     03 GRP OCCURS 10 TIMES.
        05 P1.
           07 FILLER PIC X(34).
           07 XNUM PIC 9(6).
           07 FILLER PIC X(80).
  ...
  MOVE ZERO TO XNUM (2).
  LP. ADD 1 TO XNUM (2). IF XNUM (2) LESS THAN 5 GO TO LP. DISPLAY XNUM (2). STOP RUN.
Result: subscript (1) TERMINATES (DISPLAY 5); subscript (2) and (3) are INFINITE (never reach DISPLAY).

ROOT CAUSE (from IR dump of the (2) repro): the SAME field XNUM(2) is lowered with TWO different offsets:
  - CORRECT: offset = field_off(34) + (sub-1)*stride(120)  -> BINOP -, BINOP * 120, BINOP + 34 -> 154 for occ 2 (e.g. the ADD read/write).
  - BUGGY: bare CONST 34 (no subscript arithmetic) -> LOAD_REGION offset 34 == occurrence 1 (e.g. the relational comparison's operand load).
So ADD 1 TO XNUM(2) updates offset 154, but the loop's comparison reads offset 34 (occurrence 1, always 0) -> condition never true -> infinite. Invisible for occurrence (1) because (1-1)*stride = 0, so bare-34 coincidentally equals the correct offset (that is why SQ102A's occ-(1) write loop completed while its occ-(2) verify loop produced wrong counts / FAILs).

IMPACT:
  - Directly causes the ~18 header-only infinite-loop programs (relative + sequential/indexed verify loops).
  - Likely also behind a chunk of the 77 'completes-but-FAILs' programs (silent wrong data on occ>=2 reads).

FIX DIRECTION: find the lowering path(s) that emit a bare field offset for a subscripted reference (offset omits the (subscript-1)*stride term) and make them use the same strided offset computation as the correct path. Candidates: the relational/condition operand load (interpreter/cobol/condition_lowering.py) vs the arithmetic operand handling — they resolve subscripted field offsets differently. Add a regression test from the minimal repro above (occ (1),(2),(3) all terminate and DISPLAY 5).

Related: red-dragon-m0oa.7 (parent symptom), red-dragon-m0oa (epic). Prior structured-subscripts work: red-dragon-z6ad / 6ddr.
close_reason: Verified covered + green: tests/integration/test_cobol_subscript_occurrence.py covers all 3 fixed paths — TestSubscriptedOccurrenceIncrement (occ 1/2/3 GO TO loop, the infinite-loop repro), TestSubscriptedOccurrenceConditionOperands.test_numeric_display_operand_vs_char_literal (relation-operand path), and test_ref_mod_operand (ref-mod operand path). 8/8 pass. Fixes shipped in commits 0890df65 + 41d1f384.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
FIXED (relational/condition path) + regression test — commit 0890df65 on main, via real hooks (full suite green).

Fix: interpreter/cobol/condition_lowering.py::_lower_expr_dict now passes the operand's subscripts (via expr_from_dict) to resolve_field_ref, matching the typed lower_expr_node path. The dict DID carry subscripts ({'kind':'ref','name':'XNUM','subscripts':[{'kind':'ref','name':'2'}]}); the loader was discarding them and resolving the bare (occurrence-1) offset.

Verified: minimal repro now terminates for occ 1/2/3 (was infinite for >=2); RL104A, RL111A, IX104A, SQ114A now run to END OF TEST (were infinite/header-only). COBOL suite 275 green; full default suite green via hooks.

Regression test: tests/integration/test_cobol_subscript_occurrence.py — increments XNUM(n) in a GO TO loop, asserts the correct occurrence settles at 5. Confirmed red without the fix (occ 2/3 fail), green with it.

RESIDUAL (follow-up audit, same bug class — other condition-path callers that resolve_field_ref WITHOUT subscripts): _lower_ref_mod_operand (~L667, ref-mod operand on occ>=2), and the comparison paths around L847/854. _field_byte_length (L369) and figurative-sizing callers are subscript-independent (byte_length only) and fine. Recommend auditing the ref-mod operand path next.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
AUDIT COMPLETE + residual gaps fixed (commit 41d1f384, real hooks green).

Audited all 8 subscript-omitting resolve_field_ref calls in condition_lowering.py:
- BENIGN (occurrence-independent metadata only): _field_byte_length (byte_length), _is_zoned_display_field / _is_numeric_field / _is_alphanumeric_field (category). No change.
- REAL GAPS (decode field value via offset_reg, subscript dropped → reads occurrence 1), each verified with a RED test then fixed:
    * _lower_relation_operand zoned-display-vs-alphanumeric branch (e.g. IF N(2) = "200" / = SPACES).
    * _lower_ref_mod_operand (FIELD(n)(s:l) for n>=2).
  Both now build subscripts via expr_from_dict and pass to resolve_field_ref.
- NOT A GAP: _lower_condition_str flat-string path (L849/856) — verified unreachable for subscripted refs (structured path handles them; text path can't parse NAME(sub)). No failing test constructible.

Regression tests in tests/integration/test_cobol_subscript_occurrence.py (5 total: increment loop occ 1/2/3 + relation-operand occ2 + ref-mod occ2); all confirmed red without the respective fix, green with it.

Side finding (separate, not subscript-related): sign condition 'IF N IS POSITIVE' raises KeyError 'left' in sign-condition lowering — worth its own ticket.

Considered fully fixed for the occurrence-subscript class. NIST infinite-loop programs unblocked by the earlier core fix (0890df65); these two close the remaining same-class operand paths.

title: Shared frontend keystone: typed literal + ref emission in _base.py / context.py / common
description:
PREREQUISITE for all per-frontend stories. Migrate the SHARED frontend emission
layer so every frontend can route through typed helpers.

Files & known sites (verify with grep before editing):
- interpreter/frontends/_base.py:
  - `_lower_const_literal(node)` (~479): emits `Const(value=self._node_text(node))`
    — the generic literal. Replace with TYPED helpers, e.g. `_lower_int_literal`,
    `_lower_float_literal`, `_lower_string_literal(node, value)` (value already
    unquoted by the frontend), so callers pick the kind. DECISION NEEDED: typed
    helper methods vs a `_lower_const_literal(node, literal_type)` param — pick the
    cleaner; document it so per-frontend stories follow one pattern.
  - `_lower_canonical_none/true/false` (~489/497/505): null -> Const.null_;
    true/false -> Const.bool_(reg, True/False). Mechanical.
  - default-return / array size+index sites (~850, 1085, 1177, 1199, 1211, 1248,
    1380): size/index "0"/"1"/str(i) -> Const.int_; NONE_LITERAL -> Const.null_.
- interpreter/frontends/context.py:
  - emit_func_ref (~234, builds `Const(value=str(func_label))`) -> Const.func_ref.
  - emit_class_ref (~254) -> Const.class_ref.
- interpreter/frontends/common/ (patterns.py ~10 sites, expressions.py ~7): grep
  `Const(` and migrate.

Output of this story: typed base helpers + a documented convention for how a
frontend declares a literal's type, which all per-frontend stories then use.

Acceptance: _base.py/context.py/common emit only typed Consts; the migration
convention is documented in this story's notes; at least one already-migrated
frontend (e.g. python, done in its own story) exercises the new base helpers green.

Parent: red-dragon-gjoy.
notes:
CROSS-CUTTING FINDINGS from all 15 frontend analyses (gjoy.4-17):

1. THE real shared keystone is `interpreter/frontends/common/expressions.py::lower_const_literal`
   (plus _base.py `_lower_const_literal`/`_lower_canonical_*` and context.py
   emit_func_ref/emit_class_ref). `lower_const_literal` stores raw `node_text` and is
   reached by nearly every frontend's dispatch table for NUMBER/STRING/etc. nodes.

2. DESIGN DECISION (resolve here, all frontend stories depend on it): make literal
   typing per-language. Recommended: each frontend's dispatch table re-points its
   NUMBER/STRING/CHAR/etc. node types to LANGUAGE-SPECIFIC typed lowerers (calling
   Const.int_/float_/string/...), because numeric/string/char syntax differs per
   language. The shared layer provides only the unambiguous helpers: canonical
   none->Const.null_, true/false->Const.bool_, func/class refs. Avoid a single
   mega lower_const_literal trying to handle all languages.

3. RECURRING per-language hazards the typed lowerers MUST handle (do NOT use naive
   int()/float()/strip):
   - Integer bases: hex/octal/binary/underscored → `int(text, 0)` after removing
     digit separators and type suffixes (Java L, C/C++ u/l/ll, C# L/U/m, Rust i32/u64,
     Kotlin L/u, JS/TS bigint `n`, C++14 `'` separators).
   - int vs float distinction where the language has both (Lua 5.3+, Java, etc.);
     hex floats (Lua `0x1p4`), trailing-dot floats, exponents.
   - String UNQUOTING + escapes, per language: single vs double quote rules (PHP,
     Ruby, Pascal `''`), raw strings (Rust `r#"..."#`, C++ `R"(...)"`, Go backtick,
     Lua long-bracket `[[ ]]`/`[=[ ]=]`), interpolation fragments (PHP/Ruby/JS/Kotlin/
     Scala) where STRING_CONTENT children are already unquoted and must NOT be re-stripped.
   - CHAR literals → integer ord: C, C++, Java, Go (rune), Pascal (`#65`/`'x'`),
     with full escape resolution (`\n`,`\0`,`\xNN`,`\uXXXX`). These are int, not string.
   - Null/default-return divergence: C default return is int 0 (NOT null); Scala Unit
     `()` → Const.null_; most others NONE_LITERAL → Const.null_.
   - Ruby symbols `:foo` are colon-prefixed strings — must NOT be quote-stripped.
   - Negative literals (Rust NEGATIVE_LITERAL) may be a single token or unary-minus +
     child — verify node shape before parsing.

4. COORDINATION: TypeScript reuses JavaScript lowerers directly (imports lower_js_*).
   JS (gjoy.5) and TS (gjoy.9) must be migrated together / TS tests run after JS.

5. `lower_const_literal` is reached as a FALLBACK by java/cpp field-access/cast paths;
   once Const requires type_expr it raises immediately — so this shared helper must be
   migrated (or removed) before ANY frontend can go green.
KEYSTONE CONVENTION (set here, all frontend stories follow):

Literal typing is PER-LANGUAGE. The shared layer provides typed building-block helpers; frontends pick the right one once they know the literal kind.

SHARED HELPER API (interpreter/frontends/common/expressions.py):
  lower_int_literal(ctx, node, *, text=None)   → Const.int_
    Strips _ separators, uses int(text, 0) for base handling.
    Pass text= when caller has stripped lang suffixes (Java L, C u/l, Rust i32/u64, Kotlin L/u, JS/TS n, C++ tick separators).
  lower_float_literal(ctx, node, *, text=None) → Const.float_
    Pass text= when caller strips trailing f/F etc.
  lower_string_literal(ctx, node, value)       → Const.string
    value MUST BE ALREADY-UNQUOTED str. Per-language unquoting stays in frontend story.
  lower_null_literal(ctx, node)                → Const.null_
  lower_bool_literal(ctx, node, value: bool)   → Const.bool_
  lower_canonical_none/true/false(ctx, node)   → typed wrappers for dispatch tables

lower_const_literal FATE: RAISES TypeError with clear message directing to typed helpers.
_lower_const_literal in _base.py also raises. Frontend stories MUST re-point their dispatch tables to the appropriate typed helper.

FALLBACK SITES: lower_paren/spread_arg/attribute/subscript/update_expr malformed-node branches now emit Symbolic (not Const) — semantically correct and type-safe.

DEFAULT_RETURN_VALUE: Always CanonicalLiteral.NONE across all frontends (no overrides found). All implicit-return/throw default sites emit Const.null_.

CONTEXT.PY REFS: emit_func_ref → Const.func_ref; emit_class_ref → Const.class_ref(class_type=UNKNOWN).

COMMON FILES MIGRATED: expressions.py, patterns.py, assignments.py, declarations.py, exceptions.py, default_params.py, _base.py, context.py.
close_reason: Shared typed literal/ref helpers (lower_int/float/string/null/bool_literal + lower_default_return preserving per-language default_return_value); migrated all unambiguous shared Const sites in _base.py/context.py/common/; lower_const_literal now raises directing to typed helpers. 23 unit tests green. Convention documented in note. Default-return per-language bug found+fixed in review. Commit 76b7affe.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
CORRECTION: default_return_value is NOT always CanonicalLiteral.NONE. Two frontends use non-null sentinels:

- C (interpreter/frontends/c/frontend.py:60): default_return_value="0" — C bare 'return;' routes to common/assignments.py::lower_return, emitting int 0, not null.
- Scala (interpreter/frontends/scala/frontend.py:40) and Rust (interpreter/frontends/rust/frontend.py:56): default_return_value="()" — implicit fall-off-end return routes to common/declarations.py::lower_function_def, emitting string "()", not null.

FIX APPLIED (commit amends 10dc0fd2): Added lower_default_return(ctx, node, sentinel) to interpreter/frontends/common/expressions.py. It mirrors _parse_const classification (None->null_, int->Const.int_, float->Const.float_, else->Const.string) so behavior is IDENTICAL to pre-migration. The two shared sites (lower_return no-value branch and lower_function_def implicit fall-off-end) now call lower_default_return(ctx, node, ctx.constants.default_return_value) instead of hardcoded Const.null_.

Frontend stories that should verify their default-return behavior: C (gjoy.11), Scala (gjoy.1.1), Rust (gjoy.15).

title: Typed Const literals: migrate all frontends to typed Const factories
description:
EPIC. Complete the typed-Const refactor (red-dragon-v0l2) across all language
frontends. The IR Const now carries a REQUIRED, non-optional TypeExpr (no
_parse_const guessing). Core infrastructure + COBOL + the LLM/text wire path are
DONE on branch `typed-const-literals` (commits up to a7c00456):
  - Task 1: FoundationTypeName.NULL + canonical NULL scalar.
  - Task 2: Const.type_expr required (kw_only) + typed factories
    (int_/float_/string/bool_/null_/func_ref/class_ref) + typed _handle_const.
  - Task 3: LLM/text wire path — CONST carries `literal_type`; _const builds typed.
  - Task 4: all COBOL producers migrated; _status_const_reg removed; parse_literal
    quoted-alphanumeric bug fixed.

REMAINING (this epic): migrate the 15 non-COBOL frontends + shared layers +
non-handler consumers, then delete _parse_const. The branch suite stays RED until
the whole epic completes (atomic, required-field design — main is untouched).

KEY DIFFICULTY (why per-frontend stories): literal emission is NOT a mechanical
find-replace. The shared base `interpreter/frontends/_base.py::_lower_const_literal`
emits `Const(value=self._node_text(node))` — the literal's SOURCE TEXT — and relies
on _parse_const to infer type. Migrating requires:
  - typed base helpers (int/float/string/bool/null literal lowering),
  - each frontend routing its literal dispatch to the right helper by node type,
  - per-language STRING unquoting/escape handling (today downstream of _parse_const's
    quote-strip),
  - func/class ref emission via Const.func_ref/class_ref (shared context.py
    emit_func_ref/emit_class_ref).

Each child story exhaustively analyses ONE frontend: every Const(...) site with
file:line and intended type, the literal-dispatch path + node types, string/escape
handling, what must change, and the test command to prove that language green.

Definition of done for the epic: all frontends migrated, consumers migrated,
_parse_const deleted, FULL suite green, branch merged to main.

Related: red-dragon-v0l2 (the design/root issue). Spec:
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-16-typed-const-literals-design.md. Plan:
docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-16-typed-const-literals.md.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text: EPIC COMPLETE. All 15 frontends + scala + shared layers migrated to typed Const; non-handler consumers migrated (gjoy.2); _parse_const deleted (gjoy.3). Branch typed-const-literals green on real hooks. Ready to merge to main.

title: Many NIST programs halt after printing the CCVS header — full validation loop not executed
description:
DISCOVERED 2026-06-16 after fixing the WRITE-contents bug (red-dragon-m0oa.6).

With WRITE now flushing real record contents, NIST print files contain genuine
CCVS report text — but many programs emit ONLY the header block and stop, never
producing per-test detail lines or the END-OF-TEST summary. So assert_nist_pass
(which only checks for absence of "FAIL*") still passes vacuously for these.

Measured record counts (max_steps=200_000, so NOT a step cap):
  SQ102A: 13 records, no end-summary  (header only)
  SQ103A: 25 records, no end-summary
  IX101A: 16 records, no end-summary
  RL101A: 34 records, END summary PRESENT  <-- runs to completion

So SOME programs complete (RL101A) and most do not. The incomplete ones print
HEAD-ROUTINE + COLUMN-NAMES-ROUTINE then stop before the test body emits detail
lines. Cause unknown — candidates: an unhandled statement/feature in the test
loop silently halts execution; a PERFORM/GO TO target not resolved; a control-flow
fall-through to STOP RUN; or a runtime error swallowed by the engine.

INVESTIGATION NEEDED:
- Trace SQ102A after the header: which paragraph executes last before halt?
  Compare against RL101A (which completes) to isolate the divergence.
- Determine whether execution stops (no more steps) or loops/exits early.

HARNESS WEAKNESS (related): tests/nist/conftest.py assert_nist_pass only checks
`"FAIL*" not in records`. It should ALSO require evidence the program ran to
completion — e.g. the CCVS end-of-test summary line — so "halted after header"
cannot masquerade as a pass. Strengthening this will turn the currently-hollow
passes into real failures that reveal the halt bug above. Decide with maintainer
before flipping, as it will (correctly) fail many currently-"passing" programs.

Related: red-dragon-m0oa (epic), red-dragon-m0oa.6 (WRITE-contents fix, prerequisite).
notes: Conformance checkpoint 2026-06-19 after USE declaratives (m0oa.4 closed): NIST -m nist -p no:randomly = 36 passed / 117 failed / 9 skipped (1M step budget). Program-level count flat vs prior ~37; USE declaratives resolve declarative-execution assertions (SQ103A SEQ-TEST-GF-10) but those programs stay red on other gaps. Remaining program-level blockers tracked under this epic.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
REDIAGNOSIS (2026-06-18, evidence-based) — the 'halt after header' premise is WRONG. Programs do NOT halt or infinite-loop; they are STEP-STARVED.

Evidence (SQ102A):
- Completes with END OF TEST at max_steps=600_000 (records=33) and 1_200_000 (records=37). The NIST harness budgets only max_steps=50_000.
- At 119_422 steps it is at iteration ~735 of the 750-iteration SEQ-TEST-WR-01 write loop and has NOT reached the read phase (SEQ-INIT-GF-02). Hottest blocks: para_SEQ-TEST-WR-01-LOOP (80_892 step-rows), write_after_106 (33_000). So ~163 VM steps PER record write.
- Root cause of the step blowup: numeric/group operations on REGION-stored DISPLAY fields lower to huge instruction sequences. 'ADD 1 TO XRECORD-NUMBER(1)' (PIC 9(6) at offset 34 in a subscripted OCCURS group) → ~30 per-digit __list_get/__nibble_set/__list_set ops. 'MOVE FILE-RECORD-INFO-P1-120(1) TO SQ-FS1R1' (120-byte group copy) → LOAD_REGION→__bytes_to_string→__string_to_bytes→__make_list→__list_concat→__list_slice→WRITE_REGION. 750 records × this cost ≈ 250k-600k steps.

Confirmed in isolation that subscripted ADD, deep nesting, non-zero field offset, region storage via group-MOVE, and WRITE-in-loop each work correctly and terminate — the loop is NOT logically broken, just expensive.

Implication: there are TWO real problems, neither is a halt:
1. Inefficient lowering of region byte/numeric ops (~10-100× more instructions than needed). This is the high-value fix — a direct region→region copy builtin and a compact DISPLAY-numeric increment would cut per-record cost dramatically and let many programs complete within a sane budget.
2. The harness max_steps=50_000 is far below what file-heavy CCVS programs need even when efficient.

Note: SQ102A at 600k completes but reports real FAILs (FAIL present in output) — a separate conformance-correctness issue, distinct from the step problem.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
RESULT — raised NIST harness max_steps 50_000 → 1_000_000 and ran the full suite to natural conclusion (3.5 min, xdist-parallel).

CONFORMANCE: 35 passed (was 17), 9 skipped (M-stubs), 118 failed. Raising the budget alone DOUBLED true passes — ~18 programs were failing purely on the step cap, not bugs.

The 118 failures triage cleanly:
- 77 COMPLETED-BUT-FAILED — program runs to END OF TEST but the CCVS summary reports real conformance failures. Distribution: 19 report 1 fail, 19 report 3 fails, 10 report 2 fails, then a long tail (4/6/7/8/9/12/14… up to two programs reporting 521). These are GENUINE feature/semantics bugs — the real conformance work.
- 35 INCOMPLETE (no END OF TEST even at 1M), sub-split:
  * 18 HEADER-ONLY (stop at 13 records) — halt EARLY and never progress despite 1M steps. This is the true 'halt after header' bug, now isolated to ~18 programs (not the ~98 originally claimed). Likely an unimplemented statement / control-flow dead-end. Highest-value halt bug.
  * 5 RUNAWAY OUTPUT — write absurd record counts then still no summary: IX102A (2289), SQ201M (30970), SQ208M (31144), SQ209M (31149), SQ210M (31155). Smell like runaway/near-infinite write loops (real bug, distinct from step-starvation).
  * 12 PARTIAL (18–291 records) — stop partway; mix of genuinely step-heavy (needs >1M) and possible bugs.

So the original m0oa.7 framing ('many halt after header') was mostly step-starvation; the genuine early-halt bug is now ~18 programs. Suggest re-scoping: (a) chase the 18 header-only halts, (b) the 5 runaway-output programs, (c) the 77 completes-but-fails as the conformance backlog, (d) the perf work (region-op lowering, see prior comment) remains valuable to bring the budget back down.

Harness change: tests/nist/test_{sq,ix,rl}.py max_steps 50_000 → 1_000_000.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text: Header-only halts investigated → ROOT CAUSE filed as red-dragon-9cxh: subscripted field access on occurrence >=2 drops the (sub-1)*stride term in some lowering paths (e.g. relational comparison reads bare offset 34 = occurrence 1), so loops keyed on XNUM(2..) never terminate → genuine infinite loops (RL104A still 13 records at 3M steps). Confirmed minimal repro: ADD 1 TO XNUM(2) loops forever, XNUM(1) works. 8/18 header-only are relative-file tests (use the (2) work area).

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text: Post-fix NIST re-measure (after red-dragon-9cxh subscript fix, commit 0890df65): 37 passed (was 35), 116 failed (was 118), 9 skipped. The 18 header-only INFINITE LOOPS are eliminated — those programs now terminate; 2 flipped to PASS and ~16 now COMPLETE-BUT-FAIL (revealing downstream conformance bugs, now diagnosable instead of hanging). So the qualitative win (no more hangs) exceeds the +2 headline. Remaining work: the completes-but-fail conformance backlog + residual subscript-dropping sites noted in red-dragon-9cxh + region-op perf.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
NIST re-measure on current main (after subscript fix 9cxh + sign-condition fix tqog), 1M budget: 37 passed, 9 skipped, 116 failed.

Progression of the hang/incomplete bucket: ~98 'halts' (50k-cap myth) -> 35 (after 1M budget) -> 21 (now). The subscript + sign fixes converted ~14 more programs from infinite-loop/crash into completing runs (completes-but-fail rose 77 -> 89). Pass count plateaued at 37 because those conversions land in completes-but-fail, not pass.

116 failures:
- 89 COMPLETES-BUT-FAIL (real conformance backlog). Fail-count distribution skewed small: 20 report 1 fail, 18 report 3, 12 report 2 -> many are 1-3 bugs from passing.
- 21 INCOMPLETE: 5 header-only (<=20 recs), 11 partial (21-1000), 5 runaway (>1000): IX102A (2250), SQ201M/208M/209M/210M (~31000 each, M-stubs) — runaway write loops, distinct bug class.

Next-target recommendation: cluster the low-fail-count completes-but-fail programs by what they assert (shared bugs likely flip many at once); then the 5 runaway-output write loops; then the 5 header-only halts.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
CCVS failure tracer built (scripts/nist_ccvs_tracer.py, commit e7c317da) — hooks apply_update to snapshot PAR-NAME/FEATURE/RE-MARK at each ERROR-COUNTER increment, then clusters. Ran across the full corpus: 153 programs, 931 failing assertions.

ROOT-CAUSE CLUSTERS (RE-MARK):
  318 INCORRECT RECORD NUMBER  — but ALL from ONE program RL213A (relative EXTEND loop, 318 sub-asserts). High assertion count, unblocks 1 program.
  242 blank/no-remark (value mismatches)
   44 WRONG LENGTH RECORD      — RL206A(22)+RL211A(22): variable-length records
   ~25 AT END / declarative not fired (NOT END PATH 13, AT END NOT TAKEN 4, DECLARATIVE NOT EXECUTED 4, UNEXPECTED NON-EXECUTION OF AT END 3) — SQ106A, SQ124A, SQ103A...
   24 CLOSE status (CLOSE/STATUS 16, UNEXPECTED ERROR CODE FROM CLOSE 8)
   ~20 indexed INVALID KEY PATH — IX212A(15), IX213A(16)
   15 INCORRECT RECORD COUNT — RL103A/110A/203A (relative)

PROGRAM-UNBLOCK PRIORITY (shared bugs across multiple programs):
 1. AT END / USE declarative firing (overlaps red-dragon-4q25.28 READ AT END + m0oa.4 USE) — most programs.
 2. Variable-length records / WRONG LENGTH RECORD (RL206A, RL211A).
 3. Indexed INVALID KEY (IX212A, IX213A).
 4. CLOSE file status.
 5. Relative EXTEND (RL213A alone, but flips it).

Incidental bugs surfaced: serialization 'name=[null]' unresolvable field ref; 'VARYING variable ODO-IX not found in layout' (PERFORM VARYING over OCCURS DEPENDING); a <run aborted: OverflowError> crash in at least one program. Worth separate tickets.

Tool reusable for the whole backlog: poetry run python scripts/nist_ccvs_tracer.py [PROG...].

title: WRITE record-name (no FROM) writes the record NAME literal, not the record contents — NIST passes are vacuous
description:
DISCOVERED 2026-06-16 while verifying red-dragon-m0oa.3.

`WRITE record-name` without a FROM clause writes the literal record-NAME string
instead of the record area's current contents.

Location: interpreter/cobol/lower_io.py lower_write (~lines 307-313):
    if stmt.from_field and ctx.has_field(stmt.from_field, materialised):
        from_ref, from_rr = ctx.resolve_field_ref(stmt.from_field, materialised)
        decoded_reg = ctx.emit_decode_field(from_rr, from_ref.fl, from_ref.offset_reg)
        data_reg = ctx.emit_to_string(decoded_reg)
    else:
        data_reg = ctx.const_to_reg(stmt.from_field or stmt.record_name)  # BUG

The else branch emits the record name as a string constant. So `WRITE OUT-REC`
writes "OUT-REC" (truncated to the record length), and `WRITE DUMMY-RECORD`
writes "DUMMY-RECORD". The preceding `MOVE x TO record` correctly populates the
record field (verified via DISPLAY), but WRITE never reads it.

Correct COBOL: `WRITE record-name` flushes the CURRENT CONTENTS of that 01-level
record area. The FROM form (`WRITE rec FROM fld`) is the only case currently
handled correctly.

EVIDENCE — SQ102A print file (a "passing" NIST program) contains:
    'DUMMY-RECORD                                ...'   (repeated, 120-byte recs)
i.e. the literal record name, NOT the real PASS/FAIL report.

CONSEQUENCE — VACUOUS NIST PASSES:
tests/nist/conftest.py assert_nist_pass only checks `"FAIL*" in record`. Since
the written records are always "DUMMY-RECORD" (or the record name), "FAIL*" can
never appear, so the assertion passes regardless of whether the program's logic
succeeded. The entire NIST "140/162 pass" figure is therefore not validating
PASS/FAIL output — it only confirms that *something* was written to PRINT-FILE.
This affects all SQ/IX/RL NIST programs, the m0oa.1 fixes (SQ152A/SQ155A), and
the m0oa.3 declaratives programs.

FIX (sketch): in lower_write (and check lower_rewrite identically), when there
is no FROM field, read and encode the RECORD-NAME's own field region:
    elif ctx.has_field(stmt.record_name, materialised):
        rec_ref, rec_rr = ctx.resolve_field_ref(stmt.record_name, materialised)
        decoded_reg = ctx.emit_decode_field(rec_rr, rec_ref.fl, rec_ref.offset_reg)
        data_reg = ctx.emit_to_string(decoded_reg)
    else:
        data_reg = ctx.const_to_reg(...)  # last-resort fallback
Mirror the same fix in lower_rewrite. Verify against the NIST harness: after the
fix, assert_nist_pass becomes meaningful and may surface real failures that were
previously masked — those are NEW findings to triage, not regressions.

ACCEPTANCE:
- `WRITE record-name` (no FROM) writes the record area contents.
- A focused integration test: MOVE a known value into an FD record, WRITE it,
  read the file back, assert the file holds that value (not the record name).
- Re-run full NIST SQ/IX/RL suite; report the REAL pass/fail/skip breakdown
  (expect some previously-vacuous passes to now fail — file those separately).

Related: red-dragon-m0oa (epic). Blocks meaningful validation of m0oa.1/m0oa.3.
close_reason: Fixed: WRITE/REWRITE without FROM now flushes the record area's contents (via _write_source_reg), not the record name. RED/GREEN proven; NIST print files now contain real CCVS text instead of 'DUMMY-RECORD'. 1580 cobol tests green. RESIDUAL: NIST passes still partly vacuous because many programs halt after the header (separate cause) — filed red-dragon-m0oa.7. Pushed.

title: NIST-85 file I/O conformance: close remaining gaps (22 non-passing programs)
description:
Umbrella epic for closing the remaining NIST-85 file I/O conformance gaps.

Baseline (full probe 2026-06-16, /tmp/nist_probe.py, not committed):
140/162 pass (86%), 0 FAIL, 19 SKIP, 3 ERROR.
- SQ (85): 79 pass
- IX (42): 34 pass
- RL (35): 27 pass

Test infra: tests/nist/test_sq.py, test_ix.py, test_rl.py (all @pytest.mark.nist,
excluded from main suite). Conftest: tests/nist/conftest.py (make_provider,
assert_nist_pass). NIST sources under
proleap-bridge/proleap-cobol-parser/src/test/resources/gov/nist/.

Remaining non-passing buckets (children below):
1. DECLARATIVES not handled — 9 programs (was mis-labeled "step-cap"; verified NOT step-cap)
2. INPUT-mode WRITE returns wrong status — 2 programs (SQ152A, SQ155A)
3. Bare PIC P clause unparsed — 1 program (IX110A)
4. External-input M-stub programs — 9 programs (need input data files; likely won't-fix)

Order of work: tackle the already-diagnosed failure cases (buckets 2, 3) first,
then DECLARATIVES (bucket 1). Bucket 4 last / may be won't-fix.

See memory: project_nist85_probe.md

title: Multi-operand DISPLAY only emits the first operand
description: DisplayStatement.from_dict kept only operands[0] (the ProLeap bridge correctly sends all operands), and lower_display printed that single operand. So any 'DISPLAY a b c ...' rendered just 'a' — e.g. db2-samples cursor.sqb's 'display pname, " in dept. ", dept, " is a manager"' emitted only the name. NOT COMP-5-related (COMP-5 displays fine alone; the abort was the dropped operands, independent of type). Fix: DisplayStatement holds a tuple of operands; lower_display lowers each to its display string and folds them with __string_concat_pair, printing once (COBOL concatenates operands onto one line). Regression test: TestDisplayMultipleOperands.
close_reason: Fixed: DisplayStatement now keeps all operands; lower_display concatenates them (string_concat_pair) and prints once. Regression test TestDisplayMultipleOperands.

title: PERFORM <section> THRU <paragraph> mis-keys return continuation on section end
description: PERFORM A THRU B where A is a SECTION and B is a PARAGRAPH that is NOT the last paragraph of A: resolve_perform_target (interpreter/cobol/lower_perform.py) keyed the return continuation on section_{A}_end instead of para_{B}_end. The THRU paragraph emits 'resume_continuation para_{B}_end', so the keys never matched and control fell through B into the following paragraph (e.g. STOP RUN) after a single iteration instead of returning to the PERFORM. Surfaced by IBM db2-samples cursor.sqb (Fetch-Loop SECTION; End-Fetch-Loop THRU target; End-Prog STOP RUN follows in same section) — the FETCH loop ran once. Fix: when THRU target is a paragraph (not in section_paragraphs), key continuation on para_{thru}_end. Regression test: tests/integration/test_cobol_programs.py::TestPerformThruParagraphInsideSection. Worked by luck previously only when the THRU paragraph happened to be the last in its section.
close_reason: Closed

title: ProLeap merges adjacent EXEC CICS statements into one AST node; second is dropped
description:
Two EXEC CICS...END-EXEC statements that are ADJACENT (no intervening COBOL statement/period) are captured by ProLeap/the bridge as a SINGLE exec node: getExecCicsText() returns the concatenation 'EXEC CICS A ... END-EXEC EXEC CICS B ... END-EXEC'. The Python frontend (interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py:1026-1027 -> parse_exec_cics_text) keeps only the FIRST verb; the second statement is silently DROPPED.

Evidence (Nantero1/zBANK, a conversational program): the bridge emits 11 EXEC_CICS nodes for 15 actual statements; 5 are merged blobs with 2x END-EXEC:
  - SEND MAP(...) ERASE  +  RECEIVE MAP(...)        -> RECEIVE dropped  (x3 screens)
  - UNLOCK DATASET(...)  +  READ DATASET(...) UPDATE -> READ dropped
  - SEND MAP(...) DATAONLY ERASE + RETURN           -> RETURN dropped
parse_exec_cics_text itself is fine: given a single well-formed 'EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP(...) INTO(...) END-EXEC' it returns verb='RECEIVE MAP'. The loss is purely the merge.

Impact: blocks ALL conversational CICS (SEND-then-RECEIVE with no statement between is the core idiom), and any code with two adjacent EXEC CICS. CardDemo (pseudo-conversational) dodges it because it always has an intervening statement/period.

Repro: drive zBANK through cicada; the strategy lowers {SEND MAP:7, UNLOCK:2, REWRITE:2} and ZERO RECEIVE/READ. Bridge JSON shows the merged exec_cics_text.

Candidate fixes:
  (A) Python-side (cheaper, no JAR rebuild): when an exec_cics_text contains more than one 'EXEC CICS ... END-EXEC' unit, split it and emit one ExecCicsStatement per unit (in cobol_statements.py construction, or have parse_exec_cics_text/cics_parser return a list).
  (B) Bridge-side (Java, needs Maven rebuild): split adjacent execCicsStatements into separate nodes during serialization, or fix the text-extraction span.

Discovered via cicada conversational spike (zBANK benchmark, cicada-lkx).
notes: ATTRIBUTION CONFIRMED: the merge is in ProLeap, not our bridge. Bridge serializeStatements() is strictly 1:1 (one JSON node per ProLeap Statement, no grouping, never drops EXEC_CICS). getExecCicsText() is ProLeap's AST method (io.proleap...execcics.ExecCicsStatement). A single node returns text spanning TWO 'EXEC CICS...END-EXEC' units, which is only possible if ProLeap built one AST node covering both -> ProLeap parses two adjacent EXEC CICS (no intervening statement/period) as a single ExecCicsStatement whose span runs to the LAST END-EXEC. Bridge JSON: 11 EXEC_CICS nodes for 16 real statements; 5 merged. Fix options: (A) Python frontend split of multi-unit exec_cics_text into N statements [cheapest, no JAR]; (B) same split in Java bridge [JAR rebuild]; (C) fix ProLeap grammar [heaviest, true fix].
close_reason: Fixed in ProLeap fork (1c10980), propagated to red-dragon (368839e2) and cicada (e23e926). Grammar: tokenise the '}'-terminated END line (EXECCICSENDLINE) separately from continuation lines and make execCicsStatement = EXECCICSLINE* EXECCICSENDLINE, so adjacent EXEC CICS split at their own END-EXEC boundary instead of EXECCICSLINE+ greedily merging them. ScopeImpl combines both token lists for getExecCicsText. New ExecCicsAdjacentTest (3 period-less adjacent -> 3 statements) green; existing period-based test + 26 CICS/SQL grammar tests pass; cicada 56 cics tests pass. zBANK now lowers all 16 statements incl 3 RECEIVE/1 READ/1 RETURN. NOTE: EXEC SQL/SQLIMS share the same EXECxLINE+ pattern and have the same latent merge bug — left as-is (out of scope), worth a follow-up.

title: COBOL: SET level-88 condition-name TO TRUE does not write to an adjacent single-byte group field (88 on 3rd of consecutive PIC X(1) flags)
description:
SET <88-condition-name> TO TRUE fails to write the 88's VALUE byte into the parent elementary field when the parent is one of several adjacent single-byte (PIC X(1)) fields inside a group. Isolated repro: a group with WS-INPUT-FLAG/WS-PFK-FLAG/WS-EDIT-ACCT-FLAG each PIC X(1); 88 FLG-ISVALID VALUE '1' on the 3rd field. After 'SET FLG-ISVALID TO TRUE' the group bytes remain x00 x00 x00 (expected the 3rd byte = 0xF1 EBCDIC '1'). The SET writeback targets the wrong offset / does not reflect on the 3rd byte.

Real CardDemo COACTVWC turn 5 (test_real_carddemo_account_view_three_reads, xfail-strict): WS-EDIT-ACCT-FLAG PIC X(1) (3rd of WS-INPUT-FLAG/WS-PFK-FLAG/WS-EDIT-ACCT-FLAG at COACTVWC.cbl:50-61). 2210-EDIT-ACCOUNT does SET FLG-ACCTFILTER-ISVALID TO TRUE ('1') for a valid id, but the byte stays at its space initial value. Then the level-88 condition-name FLG-ACCTFILTER-BLANK (88 VALUE ' ') evaluates TRUE ('   ' == ' ' under COBOL space-padded compare), driving 1300-SETUP-SCREEN-ATTRS (MOVE '*' TO ACCTSIDO) and SET NO-SEARCH-CRITERIA-RECEIVED, so the screen renders ERRMSG='No input received' / ACCTSID='*' and 9000-READ-ACCT's three chained VSAM reads never run.

Diagnosis verified by instrumenting condition lowering during the gated turn-5 run: ACCTSIDI decodes to 11 (display '00000000011'); ACCTSIDI='*' -> False, ACCTSIDI=SPACES -> False, CC-ACCT-ID=LOW-VALUES/SPACES/ZEROS -> all False; only FLG-ACCTFILTER-BLANK (parent reads 3 spaces '   ') -> True.

NOTE: This supersedes the original premise of red-dragon-dmu8, whose hypothesis (relation compare of numeric-DISPLAY field vs alphanumeric figurative/literal mis-evaluating) was disproven for the CardDemo flow — those comparisons already evaluate correctly. dmu8 was nonetheless a genuine (separate) bug for the non-numeric-content case and has been fixed (interpreter/cobol/condition_lowering.py; tests TestNumericDisplayVsAlphanumericRelation).

Likely area: SET condition-name TO TRUE lowering in the COBOL frontend (group/adjacent-field offset resolution) or the underlying STORE_FIELD offset for a single-byte elementary field within a group. Blocks durable turn 5.
close_reason: Fixed and verified. Generalizable bug (SET <88> TO TRUE on 3rd of adjacent PIC X(1) flags; digit VALUE written as char not dropped-as-int; correct parent offset) covered by 6 green tests in TestSetConditionNameToTrue (test_cobol_programs.py), incl. test_set_88_on_third_adjacent_flag_writes_at_correct_offset. Adversarial faithful-mechanism probe (SPACE-init flag + sibling BLANK 88 must read FALSE after SET) passes. Downstream CICS multi-file-read e2e scenario (the original blocker) re-run out-of-repo and is green. _set_condition_name fix at lower_arithmetic.py:1367-1391.

title: COBOL: relation compare of numeric-DISPLAY field to alphanumeric figurative/literal mis-evaluates (numeric = SPACES / = '*')
description: Comparing a USAGE DISPLAY numeric field (PIC 9(n)) to an alphanumeric figurative (SPACES) or literal ('*') in a relation condition mis-evaluates. COBOL treats the numeric-display operand by its zoned character representation for such a comparison. Real CardDemo COACTVWC 2200-EDIT-MAP-INPUTS: IF ACCTSIDI OF CACTVWAI = '*' OR ACCTSIDI = SPACES (COACTVWC.cbl:628-629) where ACCTSIDI is PIC 9(11) (generated from PICIN='99999999999'). After RECEIVE MAP correctly writes ACCTSIDI='00000000011' (verified: the bytes + ACCTSIDL=11 are written and region write-back works — turns 1/3 alphanumeric RECEIVE succeed), this condition still evaluates TRUE, so the program MOVEs LOW-VALUES TO CC-ACCT-ID, sets NO-SEARCH-CRITERIA-RECEIVED ('No input received'), and 9000-READ-ACCT (the 3 VSAM reads) never runs. Blocks durable turn 5 (test_real_carddemo_account_view_three_reads, xfail-strict). Fix in COBOL frontend condition lowering / comparison coercion (interpreter/cobol/condition_lowering.py + interpreter/types/coercion) — NOT core VM. Likely: when one relation operand is a numeric DISPLAY field and the other is alphanumeric (figurative SPACES / LOW-VALUES / char literal), compare via the numeric's zoned display representation. Companion to red-dragon-0fqr (MOVE numeric-display->alnum). Preserve numeric<->numeric and alnum<->alnum comparisons. Flip turn 5 off xfail when fixed.

comments.author: avishek-sen-gupta
comments.text:
Fixed the genuine relation-compare bug: when one operand is a numeric USAGE DISPLAY (ZONED_DECIMAL) field and the other is an alphanumeric figurative (SPACES/LOW-VALUES/HIGH-VALUES/QUOTES — not numeric ZEROS) or a quoted char literal, the field is now decoded via emit_decode_zoned_display (its zoned CHARACTER representation) and a char literal is space-padded to the field width, so both sides compare as character data. Numeric<->numeric, numeric<->numeric-literal, and alnum<->alnum comparisons are preserved. Scoped to ZONED DISPLAY (COMP/COMP-3 untouched) and unsigned-effective values (raw zoned bytes used). interpreter/cobol/condition_lowering.py; tests tests/integration/test_cobol_programs.py::TestNumericDisplayVsAlphanumericRelation (6 tests).

IMPORTANT: the original premise — that this mis-evaluation blocks CardDemo turn 5 — was DISPROVEN. With ACCTSIDI holding digits '00000000011', the conditions ACCTSIDI='*' / =SPACES already evaluated correctly (False) before this fix (int!=str). The dmu8 bug only manifests when the zoned field holds non-numeric bytes (e.g. the BMS placeholder '*' + blanks), which is now fixed. Turn 5 is actually blocked by red-dragon-0sq2 (SET 88 condition-name TO TRUE writeback to an adjacent single-byte group field).

title: COBOL: MOVE numeric-DISPLAY to alphanumeric loses leading zeros/width (decodes to int)
description: MOVE of a USAGE DISPLAY numeric field (PIC 9(n)) to an alphanumeric field (PIC X(n)) must move the sending field's character (zoned) representation left-justified, preserving leading zeros and width. Today lower_move decodes the numeric source to an int and emit_to_string drops the leading zeros and width: MOVE WS-NUM (PIC 9(11) VALUE 11) TO WS-ALPHA (PIC X(11)) yields '11' + 9 spaces instead of '00000000011'. Surfaced in real CardDemo COACTVWC 2200-EDIT-MAP-INPUTS: MOVE ACCTSIDI (PIC 9(11), generated from PICIN='99999999999') TO CC-ACCT-ID (PIC X(11)); the mangled value fails the subsequent blank/NUMERIC edit, sets INPUT-ERROR, and 9000-READ-ACCT (the 3 VSAM reads) never runs -> blocks durable turn 5 (currently xfail-strict). Fix in the COBOL frontend (lower_move / emit_decode_field / byte_builtins / types.coercion) — NOT core VM/ir/run/executor. Preserve numeric->numeric (value, target-width zero-pad), alphanumeric<->alphanumeric, and signed-overpunch handling; scope to DISPLAY (zoned) numeric sources (COMP/COMP-3 are separate categories). Flip tests/integration/cics/test_carddemo_signon_real.py::test_real_carddemo_account_view_three_reads off xfail when fixed.
close_reason: MOVE numeric-DISPLAY(zoned) -> alphanumeric now preserves zoned digit chars/width (emit_decode_zoned_display in emit_context; lower_move gates on source ZONED_DECIMAL + alphanumeric target, no ref-mod). numeric->numeric / alnum / ref-mod paths unchanged; signed deferred. Commit 35264427; full suite 14064 green. (Turn 5 still xfail on a separate RECEIVE-MAP input-delivery gap.)

title: ProLeap bridge: quoted-literal COPY 'NAME' fails to resolve NAME.cpy (literal finder ignores extension, unlike word finder)
description:
Root cause: LiteralCopyBookFinderImpl (quoted COPY path) matches the candidate's FULL path including the .cpy extension. For our bridge usage (Main.java sets setCopyBookDirectories + setCopyBookExtensions), the executing branch is isMatchingCopyBookAbsolute: it builds Paths.get(cobolCopyDir, identifier) and compares equalsIgnoreCase against the candidate absolute path. Candidate is .../cpy/CSSTRPFY.cpy, identifier is CSSTRPFY -> compares .../cpy/CSSTRPFY vs .../cpy/CSSTRPFY.cpy -> never equal because of the .cpy suffix. isMatchingCopyBookRelative has the same blind spot (endsWith of identifier with no extension vs candidate path with extension).

By contrast CobolWordCopyBookFinderImpl (unquoted COPY NAME) strips the extension via FilenameUtils.getBaseName(candidate.getName()) and matches CSSTRPFY.cpy against CSSTRPFY fine.

Fix location: proleap-bridge/proleap-cobol-parser/src/main/java/io/proleap/cobol/preprocessor/sub/copybook/impl/LiteralCopyBookFinderImpl.java. Make extension-tolerant, mirroring the word finder (compare base name of candidate vs final segment of identifier, case-insensitive), without over-matching (no substring matches). Fix the branch that actually runs (absolute), keep relative consistent.

Blocked programs: CardDemo COACTVWC.cbl (line 913 COPY 'CSSTRPFY'), plus COCRDLIC/COCRDUPC/COACTUPC/COCRDSLC which also use quoted COPY.

Requires rebuild of the vendored ProLeap parser module + shaded bridge JAR so the patched class lands in proleap-bridge/target/proleap-bridge-0.1.0-shaded.jar. JAR is gitignored.

TDD: failing Java unit test in vendored parser test tree first.

title: VM: raise IncompleteExecutionException when max_steps is exhausted mid-execution
description:
execute_cfg / execute_cfg_traced (interpreter/run.py) run 'for step in range(config.max_steps):' and, when the step budget is exhausted WITHOUT the program reaching a natural termination (end-of-CFG with no successors, or a top-level RETURN/_StopExecution), simply fall through the loop and return ExecutionStats(steps=max_steps, ...) with the partial VMState. This SILENTLY truncates execution: a program that needed more steps looks like it completed normally, so callers/tests read a partial result as success. (This already caused at least one false positive — see the 4q25.34 max_steps=500 exhaustion noted in project history.)

Desired: when the loop exhausts max_steps and there is still work to do (i.e. it did not break via a natural termination), raise an IncompleteExecutionException (new exception type) carrying the step count / current label / ip, instead of returning silently. A natural completion (hit the no-successors break or top-level RETURN) must NOT raise.

Implementation sketch: track whether the loop exited via natural termination (e.g. for/else, or a 'completed' flag set at the break sites at run.py ~394/~453 and ~552/~619). If the for-range exhausts without that flag, raise IncompleteExecutionException(steps, current_label, ip). Apply to BOTH execute_cfg and execute_cfg_traced. Define the exception in a sensible core module (e.g. interpreter/exceptions.py or alongside ExecutionStats). 

Caveat: some existing tests/callers may rely on max_steps truncation as a loop bound and would now raise — audit and fix those (raise the limit, or assert the exception) since silent truncation was masking incompleteness. The CICS dispatcher passes large max_steps (50k-400k); a genuine runaway should surface loudly rather than return a misleading partial VM.
acceptance_criteria: A program that exceeds max_steps without terminating raises IncompleteExecutionException (with step/label/ip context); a program that terminates naturally within budget does not; both execute_cfg and execute_cfg_traced behave consistently; existing callers/tests updated.

title: COBOL: multi-target MOVE drops extra receiving fields (MoveStatement.from_dict reads only operands[1])
description: MOVE x TO a b c keeps only the first receiving field: MoveStatement.from_dict reads operands[1] only, though the bridge emits all targets. COBOL MOVE distributes the source to EVERY receiving field. Surfaced by 'MOVE FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(...) TO WS-USER-ID CDEMO-USER-ID' (CDEMO-USER-ID lost). Fix: parse all receiving operands and emit a write per target.
acceptance_criteria: MOVE x TO a b c writes x into a, b, and c.
close_reason: MOVE x TO a b c now writes every receiving field. MoveStatement holds targets: list; lower_move evaluates source once and loops targets (each with own ref-mod + PICTURE conversion). Bridge already emitted all targets. Unit + JAR-gated e2e tests; full suite green.

title: COBOL: intrinsic FUNCTION support missing (UPPER-CASE, CURRENT-DATE) — bridge emits concatenated text, no FUNCTION node, no VM intrinsics
description: MOVE FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(USERIDI OF COSGN0AI) TO WS-USER-ID: the bridge serializes the source via extractValueStmtText=ctx.getText() (concatenated, no intrinsic handling) -> unresolvable name 'FUNCTIONUPPER-CASE(USERIDIOFCOSGN0AI)'; lower_move treats it as a literal. There is no FUNCTION node in cobol_expression.py and no UPPER-CASE/CURRENT-DATE intrinsic in the VM. Same root cause as the earlier FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE garbled-output gap (POPULATE-HEADER-INFO). Fix spans: bridge (emit a structured FUNCTION call with args), frontend expression parsing (FUNCTION node), and VM intrinsics (UPPER-CASE, CURRENT-DATE, LOWER-CASE, etc.).
acceptance_criteria: MOVE FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(x) TO y uppercases x; FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE returns the standard 21-char value; ref-mod slices are correct.
close_reason: Intrinsic FUNCTION support (UPPER-CASE/LOWER-CASE/CURRENT-DATE): bridge serializes FUNCTION calls as structured {kind:function,name,args}; COBOL frontend lowers to CallFunction against COBOL-layer builtins (byte_builtins.py). ALL logic in interpreter/cobol/ + bridge — verified zero core-engine edits. d9hc (CURRENT-DATE) folded in. Full suite 14015 green.

title: COBOL: condition lowering silently emits constant TRUE for unparseable conditions + missing OF/figurative/abbreviated support
description: _lower_condition_str (condition_lowering.py ~308-364) splits on whitespace and, when it can't parse the result, falls through to a SILENT fallback Const(value='True') — an unparseable condition becomes unconditionally TRUE, masking errors and taking wrong branches (e.g. 'WHEN USERIDI = SPACES OR LOW-VALUES' fires even when USERIDI is non-blank). Also lacks: OF/IN-qualified operands (USERIDI OF COSGN0AI), comparison against figurative constants (= SPACES / = LOW-VALUES / = ZEROS), and abbreviated relations (= SPACES OR LOW-VALUES). Fix: support these forms; and make an unparseable condition fail loudly (or emit a never-true guard + clear warning) rather than silently TRUE.
acceptance_criteria: OF-qualified + figurative + abbreviated conditions lower correctly; an unparseable condition does NOT silently become TRUE (loud failure or warned non-match).
notes: INPUT FORM (post-lu25): the bridge now spaces relational operators but NOT logical keywords/figuratives, so a condition arrives like 'WS-X = SPACESORLOW-VALUES' (= is spaced; SPACES/OR/LOW-VALUES glued). z31u must either (a) improve bridge insertSpaces to fully tokenize all boundaries (cleaner; also helps IF; requires JAR rebuild + regression check), or (b) de-glue figurative/keyword runs in Python. Must also: remove the silent constant-TRUE fallback (emit a loud failure or never-true guard for unparseable conditions); support OF/IN-qualified operands; figurative SPACES/LOW-VALUES/ZEROS comparisons; abbreviated relations (= SPACES OR LOW-VALUES).
close_reason: Done on the INCUMBENT structured condition format (no parallel representation). EVALUATE/SEARCH WHEN now route through the existing serializeConditionNode; COMBINED/abbreviated relations (A = X OR Y) expand into the existing AND/OR tree with inherited subject; figurative constants emitted as a 'figurative' expr kind sized to the sibling field; unparseable text-fallback conditions warn + never-match instead of silently TRUE. Full suite green (13994). A first attempt that invented a parallel format was reverted.

title: ProLeap bridge: EVALUATE WHEN conditions not space-normalized (serializeEvaluate omits insertSpaces)
description: StatementSerializer.serializeEvaluate (~line 745) serializes EVALUATE WHEN conditions with raw ctx.getText() and does NOT call insertSpaces, unlike the IF and other WHEN paths (lines ~1017/1540/1554). Result: a condition like 'USERIDI OF COSGN0AI = SPACES OR LOW-VALUES' arrives space-less as 'USERIDIOFCOSGN0AI=SPACESORLOW-VALUES', which the Python condition lowering cannot parse. Fix: apply insertSpaces to EVALUATE WHEN conditions consistent with the other condition paths. Requires bridge rebuild (mvn -DskipTests package).
acceptance_criteria: EVALUATE WHEN condition text is space-normalized identically to IF; a WHEN with OF-qualified operands + figurative constants serializes parseably.
close_reason: serializeEvaluate now applies insertSpaces to EVALUATE WHEN conditions + subject, matching the IF/SEARCH paths; bridge JAR rebuilt. Relational operators are now spaced. NOTE: insertSpaces only normalizes relational ops (< > =), so figurative-constant + logical-keyword gluing (e.g. 'SPACESORLOW-VALUES') remains -> handled in z31u.

title: COBOL: SET <88-level condition-name> TO TRUE not lowered
description: SET ERR-FLG-OFF TO TRUE (where ERR-FLG-OFF is an 88-level condition name) logs 'SET target not found in layout' (lower_arithmetic) and emits nothing. SET condition-name TO TRUE should MOVE the 88's VALUE literal into its parent elementary item. Unimplemented. Surfaced driving a real CICS program; general COBOL gap.
acceptance_criteria: SET <88-name> TO TRUE moves the condition's VALUE into the parent field; a subsequent test of that 88 evaluates true.
notes:
BLOCKER for the real menu-action turn (spike 2026-06-08): COMEN01C gates input-processing on 'IF NOT CDEMO-PGM-REENTER' and sets it via 'SET CDEMO-PGM-REENTER TO TRUE'. With SET-88 a no-op, the reenter flag never gets set, so the menu re-displays forever and never processes a selected option (no XCTL to the option program). Confirmed: turn-1 returned commarea is all-zeros; turn-2 re-renders COMEN1A instead of XCTL. Fix: SET <88-name> TO TRUE must MOVE the 88's VALUE into its parent elementary item (consult ConditionNameIndex), so a subsequent 88 test reads true.
